## [00:00 - 03:00]

In this lesson, we'll be covering in more detail one of the most important steps in your collaboration workflow, mastering the follow-up. The aim of the follow-up is to move the influencer from receiving the product and or payment to posting, creating content, attending an event, or whatever you initially agreed upon. A follow-up is to gently remind the influencer that as a brand, you've fulfilled your end of the deal, sent the product and or payment, and now it's time for them to fulfill their end. Following up with an influencer is especially important in unpaid product-only campaigns, and your campaign completion rate will vary significantly upon this step. So I'm going to give you some tips to follow up in both paid and unpaid collaborations. In an unpaid product-only campaign, you can't force the influencer to post, even if it's the agreement that you made for them because they're not being paid for their creative, time and reach. Remember that influencers don't have to like your product. They may have tried it and have varying results or even a bad experience, so in this case it's likely better that they don't post. When following up, the only thing that you can do here is be polite but persistent. If you're not polite, they'll simply ignore you. In other words, make sure that your follow up is a gentle nudge rather than a pushy prod. I like to structure this like a reminder, like maybe the influencer's just forgotten to post or hasn't had time yet, so you assume positive intent here. Maybe they're a little stuck on what to post or maybe they just need a little extra motivation. So some things I might say here would be just checking in to see if you received the product, have you had a chance to have a play with it yet, or was the fit right, etc. So address the follow up with the next logical step. They've received the product, then they'll use it and capture the experience when they post. Another example could be, I can't wait to see what you create, we just love your content, so act excited, positive and complimentary. Or sorry, I know you're really busy but we're really looking forward to seeing what you create. So be understanding and acknowledge that time could be the problem for them. Another example could be just checking in to see if there's anything else you might need from our end to continue. So act like maybe you're the one to blame so that they don't feel any resentment from your end. So again, the tips are to structure your follow up like a reminder, assume positive intent, compliment them, stay positive, upbeat and energetic, and be understanding and acknowledge their effort. What you don't want to do is make the influencer think that you're pushy, rude or too much work. So be persistent but don't overwhelm them with constant communication. One way that we handle this on hay is sending an influencer an automated push notification reminder email if they've received the product but haven't posted yet. This way it comes from us as a platform rather than you as a brand.

## [03:00 - 06:02]

If none of this is working, the next step that you might want to consider is offering them an incentive to post. Like we spoke about earlier, this could be a small payment or it could be in the form of a store credit in return for the post and they could use this themselves or give it to a friend or family member. A store credit is always going to cost you less than paying them money as they'll obviously be a markup on your product. Another way to incentivize them could be offering them an affiliate link for any sales generated from their post so that they're compensated based off their results. Finally, if all else fails and you really want that post, just pay them. It's annoying but you've already put a lot of time into trying to make things work but it's not the only way to guarantee the outcome. So moving on here to following up in paid collaborations now. When following up on paid collaborations, you're able to be a lot more direct as you often have a timeline to meet and a budget to spend. In the same mind, you still need to be polite and refrain from being too demanding as this won't be beneficial to your relationship. In paid collaborations, it's still useful to send reminder style follow-ups before any key dates that you set out in your deliverables. If they're expected to submit their content to your brand for approval before it goes live, you might like to remind them the day before this as well as a reminder the day before the post is set to go live. Keep these follow-ups brief and to the point like, hey Ella, just a friendly reminder that you'll need to submit your content for feedback tomorrow before it goes live on X date and time. Can't wait to see what you've created. Even sending the influencer calendar invites with reminders can be helpful. Another place that follow-ups might be required a little bit earlier on in the workflow and paid collaborations would be before your initial outreach if you haven't received a response and are working to a timeline. With that in mind, you still need to be polite as the influencer hasn't agreed to work with you yet, but you should express that you're extremely keen to work with them for XYZ reasons and reiterate that they're still your first choice when it comes to spending your budget. Here, you could consider saying things like, the brand and I would love to invest in you, we see a lot of potential in a paid relationship between yourself and the brand, or we'd love to put some of our monthly budget behind the content you create because we know our customers will love it. To eliminate the need for another follow-up email, try putting a date that you need an answer by. This shows you're serious about the collaboration and gives you a date to move on and focus on working with other influencers if you don't hear back. You can use an app like Mail Tag for Gmail to track open so that you know if they've opened your email but haven't responded to you yet or not. Another tactic if they still haven't replied to you is asking another brand or influencer that you're in contact with to introduce you to them. Warm introductions go a long way, especially for busy people. So paid or unpaid, it's important to keep notes of your influencer outreach so that there's no double handling and the work that you're putting it as noted.

## [06:02 - 06:34]

Document the initial and secondary outreach dates and any brief notes if they do respond so that everyone participating in the campaign is clear on the communication. This will be included in the tracking spreadsheet that I've provided for you in our workflow lesson. As an action item for this lesson, I'd like you to write some sample follow-ups to send if an influencer hasn't responded to your initial outreach or has received your product but not posted yet. You can use some of my examples and rewrite them in your own words. So go ahead, do that now and I'll see you in the next lesson.
